title: Unable to disable seccomp profile for podman in `containers.conf`
body:
/kind bug

**Description**

On CentOS 7 I ran into the `seccomp` problem when starting a container:

```
# podman run --rm -ti alpine
Error: OCI runtime error: container_linux.go:380: starting container process caused: error adding seccomp filter rule for syscall bdflush: requested action matches default action of filter
```

I studied the issues, unfortunately I did not find a normal solution, I also did not find a working `seccomp.json` for CentOS 7, but the disabled seccomp will suit me:

```
podman run --rm -ti --security-opt=seccomp=unconfined alpine
```

This is how containers started to work.
But I don’t want to remember the `--security-opt=seccomp=unlimited` switch every time, I am trying to add this value to`/usr/share/containers/containers.conf` and `/etc/containers/containers.conf` 

```
sed -E 's/^#?\s*?seccomp_profile = .*/seccomp_profile = "unconfined"/' -i /usr/share/containers/containers.conf
sed -E 's/^#?\s*?seccomp_profile = .*/seccomp_profile = "unconfined"/' -i /etc/containers/containers.conf
```

But it didn't work for me
Ok, start podman with debug log level:

```
podman run --log-level=debug --rm -ti alpine
```

And I see that the configs are being read, but by default `/usr/share/containers/seccomp.json` is loaded by default.

```
msg="Reading configuration file \"/usr/share/containers/containers.conf\""
msg="Merged system config \"/usr/share/containers/containers.conf\": ... SeccompProfile:unconfined
msg="Reading configuration file \"/etc/containers/containers.conf\""
msg="Merged system config \"/etc/containers/containers.conf\": ... SeccompProfile:unconfined
msg="Loading seccomp profile from \"/usr/share/containers/seccomp.json\""

Error: OCI runtime error: time="2021-10-07T10:53:36+03:00" level=error msg="container_linux.go:380: starting container process caused: error adding seccomp filter rule for syscall bdflush: requested action matches default action of filter"
```

<details>

```
Error: OCI runtime error: time="2021-10-07T10:27:51+03:00" level=error msg="container_linux.go:380: starting container process caused: error adding seccomp filter rule for syscall bdflush: requested action matches default action of filter"
```
</details>

Okay, if the `unconfined` profile is not accepted from the config, let's create our own profile in file `/usr/share/containers/seccomp_allow.json`: 

```json
{
  "defaultAction": "SCMP_ACT_ALLOW",
  "architectures": [
    "SCMP_ARCH_X86_64",
    "SCMP_ARCH_X86",
    "SCMP_ARCH_X32"
  ],
  "syscalls": []
}

```

And make changes in config:

```
sed -E 's|^#?s*?seccomp_profile = .*|seccomp_profile = "/usr/share/containers/seccomp_allow.json"|' \
  -i /usr/share/containers/containers.conf
sed -E 's|^#?s*?seccomp_profile = .*|seccomp_profile = "/usr/share/containers/seccomp_allow.json"|' \
  -i /etc/containers/containers.conf
```

See in debug log for command `podman run --log-level=debug --rm -ti alpine`:

```
msg="Reading configuration file \"/usr/share/containers/containers.conf\""
msg="Merged system config \"/usr/share/containers/containers.conf\": ... SeccompProfile:/usr/share/containers/seccomp_allow.json
msg="Reading configuration file \"/etc/containers/containers.conf\""
msg="Merged system config \"/etc/containers/containers.conf\": ... SeccompProfile:/usr/share/containers/seccomp_allow.json
msg="Loading seccomp profile from \"/usr/share/containers/seccomp.json\""

Error: OCI runtime error: time="2021-10-07T10:53:36+03:00" level=error msg="container_linux.go:380: starting container process caused: error adding seccomp filter rule for syscall bdflush: requested action matches default action of filter"
```

This does not seem to be the expected behavior.

And only if i change `/usr/share/containers/seccomp.json` to allow all policies the container can start.

It turns out that I can not disable seccomp for spoofing by default without breaking the standard profile?

Perhaps I should somehow properly configure `seccomp.json` so that the seccomp profile continues to work on CentOS7, but I've spent two days looking for a solution and haven't been able to find a solution yet.

CentOS7 is still alive and officially supported, maybe something can be done with these? Or at least fix reading the custom seccomp profile or disabling it.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

All actions were performed as root user

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
podman version 3.0.1
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**

comments.body:
Thanks for reaching and for the details.

I cannot reproduce with Podman 3.4.  Podman 3.0 is not supported upstream, so I suggest opening a bug in the Red Hat bugzilla.

Any chance you can upgrade to CentOS 8?

I am closing the bug but we can continue the conversation.

Running into same problem on CentOS7.

Installed podman from https://download.opensuse.org/repositories/devel:/kubic:/libcontainers:/stable/CentOS_7/

Looking at https://download.opensuse.org/repositories/devel:/kubic:/libcontainers:/stable/CentOS_7/x86_64/ - it looks like a new c/common package was built for Cent 7 on September 21st (everything else dates back to March, consistent with when we deprecated the Kubic repos for Cent 7/8). I think it's a fair assumption that the newer common (and its new containers.conf) broke the six-month-old Podman we're shipping.

@lsm5 Do you know what's going on there - was there a bug that required a rebuild?
Hate to ask again, but it is still relevant. Any updates?

I will just note that the Kubic repos for Cent 7/8 are officially deprecated, so we'd recommend you move off them regardless to official distro packages (we do recognize those are rather old for Cent 7 - but frankly, we don't have the resources to maintain alternative packaging for it, given the older kernel and libraries and the complexities those cause).

Maybe it’s worth adding this information to the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md), as I understand this problem is not going anywhere, and it would be nice to describe it in order to finalize your position and possible solutions?
I don't think it is a great idea to document short comings of older distributions.

If you want to complain about a supported OS then complain to RHEL by opening a bugzilla.   This is an upstream REPO.  If you want to open a PR to fix the install text to mention that OLD OS will not get updated, then open the PR.  If you want to build and support newer versions of Podman for older OS like CentOS and RHEL then that is also up to you. 

The current upstream community does not have the people to support this at this time.

Red Hat does not support newer versions of anything on RHEL7 at this point, it will only get security fixes at this point until 2024.
